rationale: The citing paper uses Etkin, Evangelidis, and Aaker (2015) for more than background mention: it is cited to justify the claim that a splurging versus saving goal should change responses to luxury, and the same source is used for the goal-framing manipulation implemented in Experiment 5c. This prior work therefore informs both hypothesis development (H6) and the operationalization of a key moderator in the empirical tests. While it is not the core theory behind the slow-motion mechanism, it plays a meaningful functional role in the study design and downstream interpretation.
relation_description: The citing paper applies Etkin et al. (2015) to motivate and implement a splurging-versus-saving goal manipulation as a moderator of slow motion effects. It transfers that goal-framing idea to a new context (video speed and luxury perception) to test when downstream purchase desire increases.
